title: What Is a Reverse Proxy — And Why Modern Websites Depend on It
excerpt: A reverse proxy sits between visitors and your servers, improving security, performance, and scalability for modern web applications.

content.text: Modern websites rarely expose their application servers directly to the internet. Instead, they place an additional layer in front of them called a reverse proxy.
A reverse proxy acts as an intermediary between users and backend servers. It receives incoming requests and forwards them to the appropriate application server.
When a user visits a website, their request is first sent to the reverse proxy. The proxy then determines which backend server should handle the request.
The backend server processes the request and sends the response back through the proxy to the user.
One of the most common uses of a reverse proxy is load balancing. Instead of sending all traffic to one server, the proxy distributes requests across multiple servers.
Because backend servers are hidden behind the proxy, attackers cannot interact with them directly. Reverse proxies can also filter malicious traffic and enforce security rules.
Reverse proxies can cache static content, compress responses, and manage TLS encryption. This reduces load on application servers and improves response times.

Reverse proxies are a fundamental component of modern web infrastructure. By managing traffic, improving security, and optimizing performance, they enable hosting platforms to deliver fast and reliable web applications at scale.

author: Elias De Hondt
